title: Welcome to the Academy
description: A quick introduction to what you will learn and how the programme works.

screens.body:
This programme teaches you how to use AI properly — not just the basics, but the kind of skill that genuinely changes how you work, study, and think.

It is built around **three tiers**:

**Aware** — understand what AI is, what it can do, and how to have a useful conversation with it.

**Enabled** — develop real prompt engineering skills that make AI indispensable in your daily work.

**Specialist** — build with AI: APIs, tools, agents, and professional certification.

You go at your own pace. Every module you complete unlocks the next one.
screens.title: Welcome

screens.body:
The programme mixes **courses**, **challenges**, and **assessments**.

**Courses** teach you concepts — some are built into this app, others link to world-class content from Anthropic, Coursera, and GitHub.

**Challenges** ask you to apply what you have learned to something real in your life. No right answers — just practice.

**Assessments** check your understanding before you move to the next tier. You can retake them as many times as you need.

Everything is designed to build on what came before. Trust the sequence.
screens.title: How it works

screens.body:
There is no rush. The people who get the most from this programme are the ones who actually try things — not just read about them.

When a challenge asks you to use AI for something real, do it. That is where the learning happens.
screens.title: One thing before you start

inputs.label: What are you most hoping to get from learning AI?
inputs.placeholder: Be honest — there are no wrong answers here...

title: AI Tools That Accelerate Your Work
description: Before you dive into courses, know the tools that will genuinely change how you work.

screens.body: Before you go deeper into AI courses, there are three tools you should know about. Not because they are trendy — because they will genuinely save you hours every week.
screens.title: Three tools. Genuine impact.

screens.body: Fireflies records, transcribes, and summarises your meetings automatically. Every call, every discussion, every decision — captured without you taking a single note. You can search across months of conversations, find exactly who said what, and never lose a detail again. It works with Zoom, Teams, Google Meet, and most other platforms.
screens.title: Fireflies — Your meeting memory

screens.body: Wisprflow turns your spoken words into written text — fast, accurate, and everywhere on your Mac. Instead of typing a long email, you talk. Instead of writing meeting notes from memory, you dictate them while they are fresh. Most people think faster than they type. Wisprflow removes that bottleneck.
screens.title: Wisprflow — Your voice, as text

screens.body: You have already met Claude. Here is how it fits with the other two. Fireflies captures the raw material — what was said, decided, and committed to. Wisprflow captures your thoughts — quick, unfiltered, in your own words. Claude processes it all — summarises, analyses, drafts, and helps you think clearly. Together, these three tools mean you capture everything, lose nothing, and spend your time on thinking rather than typing.
screens.title: Claude — Your thinking partner

screens.body: If you do not already have these tools: Fireflies — ask the person who invited you for access. Wisprflow — available on the Mac App Store. You do not need them to continue this programme, but the sooner you start, the sooner the value compounds.
screens.title: Your next step

inputs.label: Which tool interests you most?

title: Claude 101
description: Introduction to Claude — what it is, how it works, and your first conversations.

title: What to Look For
description: Key concepts from Claude 101 — making sure the essentials have landed.

screens.body:
Before you move on, let's make sure the key concepts landed.

Claude 101 covered a lot of ground. The three things that matter most right now:

**1. Claude is a conversation, not a command.** The more context you give, the better the response.

**2. You're in control.** Claude follows your lead. If the response isn't right, refine your question — don't just accept it.

**3. AI has limits.** It can hallucinate. It has a knowledge cutoff. Always verify important facts.
screens.title: You've just completed Claude 101

screens.body: No scoring — just checking your understanding.
screens.title: Quick check

inputs.label: What's the one thing from Claude 101 that you'll remember?
inputs.placeholder: The concept that stuck with you...

inputs.label: Name one thing you want to try using Claude for this week.

title: Your First Real Conversation
description: Use Claude to help with something real this week — homework, a work task, a decision, a creative project. Paste your best exchange and reflect on what worked.

title: Assessment: AI Basics
description: Test your understanding of AI fundamentals from Claude 101.

title: Prompt Patterns That Work
description: Five prompt patterns you can use right now to get dramatically better results from Claude.

screens.body: Before you dive into the deeper courses, here are patterns that make Claude dramatically more useful. These aren't theory — they're templates you can copy and adapt today.
screens.title: 10 prompt patterns you can use right now

screens.body:
**Start with who Claude should be.**

"You are an experienced [teacher / editor / analyst / coach]. Help me with [task]."

This shapes the perspective of every response. A teacher explains differently from an analyst.
screens.title: Pattern 1: The Role

screens.body:
**Give Claude everything it needs.**

"Here's my situation: [paste details]. Given this context, [your question]."

The more relevant context you provide, the more specific and useful the response. Don't make Claude guess.
screens.title: Pattern 2: The Context Dump

screens.body:
**Tell Claude what you want back.**

"Give me this as [a bullet list / a table / a step-by-step guide / a 200-word summary]."

Format control is one of the most underused features. You don't have to accept whatever Claude gives you.
screens.title: Pattern 3: The Format Request

screens.body:
**Don't accept the first response.**

"That's good but [make it shorter / more specific / less formal / add examples]. Also [additional instruction]."

The best results come from 2-3 rounds of refinement, not one perfect prompt.
screens.title: Pattern 4: The Refinement Loop

screens.body:
**Ask Claude to challenge you.**

"Here's my [plan / essay / idea]. What are the weaknesses? What am I missing? Be direct."

This is where AI becomes genuinely powerful — as a thinking partner who spots what you can't see.
screens.title: Pattern 5: The Critique Request

screens.body: Pick two of these patterns. Use them with Claude right now on something real. Then come back.
screens.title: Now use them

inputs.label: Which patterns did you try? What happened?
inputs.placeholder: What you tried and how it went...

inputs.label: Which pattern was most useful?

title: AI Fluency: Framework & Foundations
description: Deep dive into AI frameworks — how to think about AI capabilities and limitations.

title: AI Fluency for Students
description: AI fluency tailored for students — study skills, research, and academic use cases.

title: Solve a Real Problem
description: Pick something you're genuinely stuck on. Use Claude and everything you've learned to work through it. Document your process — what you asked, how you refined, what the outcome was.

title: Assessment: Frameworks Applied
description: Demonstrate your understanding of AI frameworks through practical scenarios.

title: Build Your AI Toolkit
description: Set up Claude with custom instructions tailored to your life. Create 3 reusable prompt templates for tasks you do regularly. Test each one and refine until they work reliably.

guidance.body: You are going to set up Claude as a personal tool — something you can come back to again and again. Think of it like creating shortcuts for the tasks you do most often.
guidance.title: What is this challenge?

guidance.body: Think about 3 things you do regularly that involve writing, thinking, or organising. Here are some ideas to get you started:
guidance.title: Step 1: Pick your tasks
examples: Writing emails — especially ones you put off because they need the right tone
Summarising meeting notes or long documents
Planning meals, trips, or weekly schedules
Helping your kids with homework topics you are rusty on
Drafting social media posts or messages
Researching a purchase — comparing options, pros and cons
Preparing for a conversation you are nervous about

guidance.body: A prompt template is a reusable message you can tweak each time. The key ingredients: tell Claude WHO it should be (role), WHAT you need (task), and HOW you want it (format/tone). Here is an example:
guidance.title: Step 2: Build a prompt template for each one
examples: Example: "You are my personal email assistant. I need to write a [professional/casual/apologetic] email to [person] about [topic]. Keep it under 150 words and match my usual tone — direct but warm."

guidance.body: Try each template with a real task. If the result is not quite right, tweak the prompt — add more context, change the tone, or be more specific about what you want. The goal is to get it working well enough that you would actually use it again.
guidance.title: Step 3: Test and refine

guidance.body: Share which 3 tasks you chose, one of your prompt templates, and what happened when you tested it. What worked? What did you have to adjust?
guidance.title: What to write below

title: Quick Start: Set Up Claude for Your Life
description: 20 minutes to a Claude that actually knows you. Set up your Profile, create your first Project, and build 3 reusable prompt templates. This is where learning turns into doing.

screens.body:
You've learned what AI is, how it works, and what it can do. That's the foundation.

Now we're going to do something practical. In the next 20 minutes, you'll set up Claude so it actually knows who you are and what you need. No more generic responses. No more starting from scratch every conversation.

Three steps. All inside Claude. By the end, you'll have a personal AI setup that works for your life — not just a chatbot you ask random questions.
screens.title: You made it — now let's make Claude yours

screens.body:
This is the single most important thing you can do in Claude. Your Profile Preferences apply to EVERY conversation — they shape how Claude responds to everything.

**How to get there:**
Click your initials in the bottom-left corner of Claude → Settings → Profile.

You'll see a field: "What personal preferences should Claude consider in responses?"

**What to write:**
Tell Claude who you are, how you work, and what you need. Be specific. Here's the formula:

• Who you are (role, context)
• How you like responses (format, length, tone)
• What you use Claude for most
• Any rules (things to always or never do)
screens.title: Step 1: Set Up Your Profile

inputs.label: Draft your Profile Preferences here before pasting them into Claude. Use the formula above.
inputs.placeholder: I am a [role]. I use Claude for [tasks]. I prefer [format/tone]. Always [rule]. Never [rule].

screens.body:
Here are three real Profile Preferences to inspire yours:

**Student:**
"I'm a Year 12 student studying Biology, Chemistry and Maths. I use Claude for revision, essay planning, and understanding complex topics. Explain things simply — if I need more depth, I'll ask. When I ask for essay help, give me a structure first, not a finished essay. Test me with questions after explaining a topic."

**Professional:**
"I'm a marketing manager at a mid-size company. I use Claude for drafting emails, brainstorming campaigns, and summarising long documents. Keep responses under 200 words unless I ask for more. Use bullet points over paragraphs. Be direct — I don't need caveats or disclaimers."

**Home & Family:**
"I manage a household with two kids (ages 10 and 14). I use Claude for meal planning, homework help, travel research, and comparing products. Keep language simple and practical. When helping with homework, explain at the child's level, not mine. For recipes, always include prep time and ingredient count."

Notice the pattern: who → what → how → rules. Specific beats vague every time.
screens.title: Step 1: Examples That Work

screens.body:
Projects let you give Claude focused context for a specific area of your life. Think of it as a workspace with its own instructions.

**Already using Projects?** Skip ahead to Step 3 — you're ahead of the curve. This step is here for people who haven't discovered Projects yet.

**How to create one:**
Click the menu (top-left) → Projects → New Project.

Give it a name and add Project Instructions — these only apply inside this project's chats.

**What makes a good first project?**
Pick the one thing you use Claude for most. Not "everything" — one specific area:

• Your job ("Marketing Assistant" — knows your brand voice, your products, your audience)
• A course you're studying ("Biology Revision" — knows your syllabus, your exam board, your weak topics)
• A side project ("Wedding Planning" — knows the date, budget, guest count, venue)
• Family admin ("Family HQ" — knows the kids' schedules, dietary needs, upcoming events)
screens.title: Step 2: Create Your First Project

inputs.label: What will your first project be? Name it and write 2-3 sentences of instructions for it.

screens.body:
A prompt template is a reusable message you tweak each time. Instead of figuring out how to ask Claude from scratch, you start with a template that already works.

**The formula:**
Role + Task + Context + Format = reliable output.

**Three templates to build now:**

**Template 1 — Your most common task:**
What do you ask Claude to do most? Write an email? Summarise something? Plan something? Build a template for that.

Example: "You are my email assistant. Draft a [professional/casual/apologetic] email to [person] about [topic]. Keep it under 150 words. Match my usual tone — direct but warm."

**Template 2 — Your thinking partner:**
When do you need help thinking something through?

Example: "I need to make a decision about [topic]. Ask me 5 clarifying questions, then give me a structured pros/cons analysis with your recommendation."

**Template 3 — Your knowledge gap:**
What topic do you regularly need to understand better?

Example: "Explain [topic] to me as if I'm [level]. Start with the key concept in one sentence, then give me 3 practical examples. Tell me what most people get wrong about this."
screens.title: Step 3: Build 3 Prompt Templates

inputs.label: Write your 3 prompt templates below. You'll test them in Claude next.
inputs.placeholder:
Template 1 (most common task): ...

Template 2 (thinking partner): ...

Template 3 (knowledge gap): ...

screens.body:
In 20 minutes you've done what most people never do:

✓ **Profile Preferences** — Claude now knows who you are across every conversation
✓ **First Project** — you have a focused workspace for your most important area
✓ **3 Prompt Templates** — reusable starting points instead of blank pages

This is your AI foundation. Everything else in the Academy builds on it.

**What to do next:**
• Open Claude and test your 3 templates with real tasks
• Refine your Profile Preferences after a week — what's missing? What needs adjusting?
• Create a second Project when you find another area that needs focused context

The rest of the Enabled tier goes deeper into prompting techniques, but you're no longer starting from zero. You have a working setup that makes Claude genuinely useful right now.
screens.title: You're set up

inputs.label: Quick reflection: which of the three steps made the biggest difference? What surprised you?
inputs.placeholder: The step that changed things most was...

title: Tier Gate: Aware Completion
description: Final assessment for the Aware tier. Pass to unlock the Enabled tier.

title: Why Prompting Changes Everything
description: Welcome to the Enabled tier — where AI stops being interesting and starts being indispensable.

screens.body:
You've completed the Aware tier. You understand what AI is, what it can do, and how to have a useful conversation with it.

The Enabled tier is where AI stops being interesting and starts being indispensable.

The difference between someone who "uses AI" and someone who's genuinely AI-enabled is one thing: prompt engineering. Not as a buzzword — as a real, practical skill that changes how you work.
screens.title: Welcome to Enabled

screens.body:
In Aware, you learned to ask Claude questions.

In Enabled, you'll learn to:

**Structure complex tasks** — breaking big problems into steps that AI can handle

**Create reusable systems** — prompts and workflows you use every day

**Think in AI-native ways** — approaching problems differently because you have AI as a tool

By the end of this tier, AI won't be something you use occasionally. It'll be something you can't imagine working without.
screens.title: What changes at this level

screens.body:
The Enabled tier includes:

• A Coursera specialisation on real-world AI application
• Anthropic's own prompt engineering tutorials
• Challenges where you build your personal prompt library and AI workflow
• Assessments that prove you genuinely understand this material

This is where the real skills start. Let's go.
screens.title: What's ahead

inputs.label: What's the one thing you most want AI to help you with at this level?

title: Real-World AI for Everyone
description: Coursera specialisation covering practical AI applications across industries.

title: Build Your Personal AI Assistant
description: Create a comprehensive custom instruction set that makes Claude work like your personal assistant. Test it across 5 different real tasks. Document what works and what doesn't.

guidance.body: Custom instructions are a set of preferences and context you give to Claude before starting a conversation. They shape how Claude responds to everything you ask — like briefing an assistant on your working style, role, and priorities before they start the job.
guidance.title: What are custom instructions?
examples: "I'm a marketing manager. Keep suggestions practical and budget-conscious."
"I prefer bullet points over long paragraphs."
"Always suggest alternatives when recommending a tool or approach."

guidance.body: Claude has three places where you can personalise how it works. For this exercise, start with Profile Preferences — that's the main one:
guidance.title: Where to set this up in Claude
examples: Profile Preferences — Click your initials (bottom-left in Claude) → Settings → Profile. The field "What personal preferences should Claude consider?" applies to EVERY conversation. This is where your custom instructions go.
Memory — Settings → Capabilities → Memory. Claude builds a picture of you over time from your conversations. You can view and edit what it has stored.

Projects — Create a Project for focused work (e.g. a specific business or task). Project instructions only apply within that project's chats — useful when you need different context for different areas of your life.

guidance.body: The best custom instructions are specific, honest, and task-oriented. They tell Claude who you are, how you work, and what kind of help you actually need. Vague instructions ("be helpful") do nothing. Specific ones ("I'm a teacher who needs lesson plans for Year 9 science") change everything.
guidance.title: What makes a good AI assistant setup?

guidance.body: Here are three real examples to inspire yours:
guidance.title: Examples for different people
examples: Student: "I am a Year 12 student studying Biology, Chemistry, and Maths. Help me revise by breaking topics into key concepts, testing me with practice questions, and explaining things I get wrong. Use simple language. When I ask for essay help, give me a structure first, not a finished essay."

Young adult: "I am 24, working my first office job in marketing. Help me write professional emails (I overthink the tone), brainstorm campaign ideas, and summarise long documents. Be direct and keep things under 200 words unless I ask for more."

Professional: "I am a project manager running 3 teams. Help me write status updates, prepare for stakeholder meetings, and analyse project risks. I prefer bullet points over paragraphs. When I share meeting notes, extract action items with owners and deadlines."

guidance.body: Follow these steps to build your personal AI assistant:
guidance.title: Your 3-step process
examples: Step 1: Define your needs — What do you use AI for most? What frustrates you about generic responses? What context would help Claude do better?
Step 2: Write the instructions — Include your role, your preferences, your common tasks, and any rules (e.g. "never use jargon", "always cite sources").
Step 3: Test across 5 real tasks — Try your instructions on different types of work: writing, analysis, brainstorming, summarising, problem-solving. Refine based on what works.

guidance.body: After testing, write about what worked and what didn't. Which instructions made the biggest difference? What did you have to change? What surprised you about how Claude responded to your specific context?
guidance.title: What to write in your reflection

title: Assessment: Applied Prompting
description: Demonstrate your ability to craft effective prompts for real-world tasks.

title: Prompt Engineering Interactive Tutorial
description: Anthropic's official prompt engineering course — a hands-on Jupyter notebook tutorial. Meaty and thorough. Work through it at your own pace, focusing on the techniques most relevant to your work.

guidance.body: This is a Jupyter notebook course hosted on GitHub, not a video series. You'll read explanations and run prompt examples directly. It's substantial — plan for 2-4 hours to work through the core material. You don't need to finish it in one sitting.
guidance.title: What to expect

guidance.body: A Claude account (free or paid) and basic familiarity with prompting. No coding experience required — the notebooks handle the technical parts. You'll get the most from this if you've already completed the earlier modules in this tier.
guidance.title: What you need

guidance.body: If you're short on time, prioritise these sections:
guidance.title: Where to focus
examples: Chapter 2: Being Clear and Direct — the foundation everything else builds on
Chapter 4: Separating Data from Instructions — essential for real-world use
Chapter 7: Using Examples (Few-Shot) — the single biggest quality lever
Chapter 9: Complex Prompts — where it all comes together

title: Real World Prompting
description: Advanced prompting techniques for production-grade AI interactions.

title: Your Prompt Library
description: Build a library of 10 prompts you'll actually reuse. Categories: research, writing, analysis, decision-making, creative. Test each one on real tasks and rate their effectiveness.

title: Assessment: Prompt Engineering Mastery
description: Prove your prompt engineering skills across multiple scenarios.

title: AI in Your Workflow
description: Map your daily or weekly routine. Identify 3 tasks where AI can save time or improve quality. Build the prompts, use them for a full week, then report: time saved, quality difference, what you'd change.

title: Tier Gate: Enabled Completion
description: Final assessment for the Enabled tier. Pass to unlock the Specialist tier.

title: From User to Builder
description: Welcome to Specialist — where you stop using AI and start building with it.

screens.body:
Most people will never reach this tier. The skills you're about to develop are the same ones used by engineers at Anthropic, and by the professionals who build AI-powered products and services.

This is where you stop being someone who uses AI and become someone who builds with it.
screens.title: Welcome to Specialist

screens.body:
In Enabled, you mastered prompting — talking to AI effectively.

In Specialist, you'll learn to:

**Use the Claude API** — call Claude programmatically, not just through a chat interface

**Build with MCP** — connect Claude to real tools, databases, and services

**Create AI agents** — systems that can plan, execute, and verify their own work

**Prepare for certification** — the Claude Certified Architect credential

This is career-grade material. Take it seriously.
screens.title: What changes here

screens.body:
The Specialist tier involves code. You don't need to be an expert programmer, but you should be comfortable reading code and following technical instructions.

If you're not there yet, that's fine — the courses will teach you what you need. But know that this tier requires more effort than the previous two.
screens.title: Before you start

inputs.label: Why do you want to reach Specialist level? What will you build?
inputs.placeholder: What's driving you to go this deep...

title: Building with the Claude API
description: Official Anthropic course on building applications with the Claude API.

title: API Fundamentals (GitHub)
description: Hands-on API fundamentals from the Anthropic courses repository.

title: Challenge: Build a Claude API Script
description: Build a working script that calls the Claude API to solve a real problem.

title: Assessment: API Fundamentals
description: Demonstrate your Claude API knowledge through practical assessment.

title: Claude Code in Action
description: Learn Claude Code — AI-powered development from inside your terminal.

title: Introduction to MCP
description: Model Context Protocol — connecting Claude to external tools and data.

title: MCP Advanced Topics
description: Advanced MCP patterns — building servers, managing resources, security.

title: Tool Use (GitHub)
description: Hands-on tool use patterns from the Anthropic courses repository.

title: Challenge: Build an MCP Server
description: Build a working MCP server that connects Claude to an external data source.

title: Introduction to Agent Skills
description: Building agent skills — extending Claude with specialised capabilities.

title: Building Skills for Claude
description: Learn to build skills — reusable instruction packages that teach Claude workflows, best practices, and domain expertise. Covers the complete lifecycle from planning to testing to distribution.

screens.body:
A **skill** is a folder containing instructions that teaches Claude how to handle specific tasks or workflows.

Instead of re-explaining your preferences, processes, and domain expertise in every conversation, you teach Claude once and it applies that knowledge every time.

The only required file is **SKILL.md** — a Markdown file with YAML frontmatter. Optionally you can include:

• **scripts/** — executable code (Python, Bash, etc.)
• **references/** — documentation loaded as needed
• **assets/** — templates, fonts, icons used in output

Think of it this way: if you find yourself giving Claude the same instructions repeatedly, that should be a skill.
screens.title: What is a Skill?

screens.body:
Skills use a **three-level system** that minimises token usage while maintaining expertise:

**Level 1 — YAML frontmatter.** Always loaded into Claude's system prompt. Gives just enough information for Claude to know WHEN the skill should be used. This is the skill's "shopfront".

**Level 2 — SKILL.md body.** Loaded when Claude decides the skill is relevant to the current task. Contains the full instructions and guidance.

**Level 3 — Linked files.** Additional files in the skill folder that Claude can discover and navigate to as needed — API guides, examples, templates.

This matters because loading everything into context all the time wastes tokens and degrades performance. The best skills are lean at Level 1 and rich at Levels 2 and 3.
screens.title: Progressive Disclosure

inputs.label: Why does progressive disclosure matter?
options: It makes the skill folder smaller on disk
It minimises token usage while maintaining specialised expertise
It makes the skill load faster
It prevents other skills from loading

screens.body:
If you're already building with MCP, here's how skills fit in:

**MCP provides the professional kitchen** — access to tools, ingredients, and equipment. It connects Claude to your services (Notion, Linear, Slack, etc.) and provides real-time data access and tool invocation.

**Skills provide the recipes** — step-by-step instructions on how to create something valuable using those tools.

Without skills, users connect your MCP but don't know what to do next. Each conversation starts from scratch. Results are inconsistent because everyone prompts differently.

With skills, pre-built workflows activate automatically. Best practices are embedded in every interaction. The learning curve drops dramatically.

Even without MCP, standalone skills are powerful — they work with Claude's built-in capabilities like code execution, document creation, and analysis.
screens.title: The Kitchen Analogy

screens.body:
Every well-built skill has these properties:

**Composability.** Claude can load multiple skills simultaneously. Your skill should work well alongside others — don't assume it's the only capability available. A good skill does one thing well and doesn't try to control everything.

**Portability.** Skills work identically across Claude.ai, Claude Code, and the API. Build a skill once and it works on all surfaces without modification, provided the environment supports any dependencies your skill requires.

These two properties are what make skills genuinely powerful. A composable, portable skill is a reusable building block that compounds in value over time.
screens.title: Two Key Properties

inputs.label: Give an example of two skills that might work well together.
inputs.placeholder: e.g. A data-analysis skill + a report-generation skill...

screens.body:
Before writing any code, identify **2-3 concrete use cases** your skill should enable.

A good use case definition looks like this:

Use Case: Project Sprint Planning
Trigger: User says "help me plan this sprint"
Steps: 1. Fetch project status  2. Analyse capacity  3. Suggest priorities  4. Create tasks
Result: Fully planned sprint with tasks created

**Ask yourself:**
• What does a user want to accomplish?
• What multi-step workflows does this require?
• Which tools are needed (built-in or MCP)?
• What domain knowledge or best practices should be embedded?

Anthropic has observed three common skill categories:

**1. Document & Asset Creation** — generating consistent, high-quality output (reports, designs, code)
**2. Workflow Automation** — multi-step processes with validation gates and templates
**3. MCP Enhancement** — adding workflow guidance on top of MCP tool access
screens.title: Planning: Start with Use Cases

screens.body:
Before building, define how you'll know your skill is working. Anthropic recommends both quantitative and qualitative metrics.

**Quantitative:**
• Skill triggers on 90% of relevant queries — test with 10-20 phrases
• Completes workflow in X tool calls — compare with and without the skill
• 0 failed API calls per workflow — monitor during testing

**Qualitative:**
• Users don't need to prompt Claude about next steps
• Workflows complete without user correction
• Consistent results across sessions — run the same request 3-5 times
• A new user can accomplish the task on first try with minimal guidance

These are rough benchmarks, not precise thresholds. The goal is to have clear criteria BEFORE you start building so you know when you're done.
screens.title: Success Criteria

inputs.label: Which is a qualitative success metric?
options: Skill triggers on 90% of relevant queries

screens.body:
The YAML frontmatter is **the most important part of your skill**. It's how Claude decides whether to load your skill. Get this wrong and your skill never activates.

**Critical rules:**
• File must be exactly **SKILL.md** (case-sensitive — not SKILL.MD or skill.md)
• Folder name uses **kebab-case** (my-cool-skill, not My Cool Skill or my_cool_skill)
• **No README.md** inside the skill folder — all docs go in SKILL.md or references/

**Required fields:**

**name** — kebab-case, no spaces or capitals, should match folder name

**description** — the field that makes or breaks your skill. Must include BOTH:
  — What the skill does
  — When to use it (trigger conditions)
  — Under 1024 characters, no XML angle brackets

**The description formula:**
[What it does] + [When to use it] + [Key capabilities]
screens.title: The YAML Frontmatter

screens.body:
Let's put theory into practice. Here's how to create a working skill from scratch.

**Step 1 — Create the folder:**
In your project directory (or ~/.claude/skills/ for global skills), create a folder in kebab-case:

my-first-skill/
  SKILL.md

**Step 2 — Add the frontmatter:**
Open SKILL.md and paste this minimal template:

---
name: my-first-skill
description: "[What it does]. Use when [trigger phrases]. Capabilities: [key features]."
---

**Step 3 — Write your instructions:**
Below the frontmatter, add Markdown instructions. Start simple — even 5 lines of clear guidance is a working skill.

**Step 4 — Test it:**
Ask Claude something that should trigger your skill. Did it activate? Did it follow your instructions? If not, refine the description or instructions and try again.
screens.title: Try It: Create Your First SKILL.md

inputs.label: What did you name your skill folder, and what does your description field say?

screens.body:
The description field is where most skills succeed or fail. Here are real examples:

**Good — specific and actionable:**
"Analyses Figma design files and generates developer handoff documentation. Use when user uploads .fig files, asks for design specs, component documentation, or design-to-code handoff."

**Good — includes trigger phrases:**
"Manages Linear project workflows including sprint planning, task creation, and status tracking. Use when user mentions sprint, Linear tasks, project planning, or asks to create tickets."

**Bad — too vague:**
"Helps with projects."

**Bad — missing triggers:**
"Creates sophisticated multi-page documentation systems."

**Bad — too technical, no user triggers:**
"Implements the Project entity model with hierarchical relationships."

The test: would a user actually say something that matches your description? If not, Claude won't know when to load your skill.
screens.title: Good vs Bad Descriptions

inputs.label: Write a skill description for a tool that helps create meeting agendas. Include what it does AND trigger phrases.

screens.body:
After the frontmatter, the SKILL.md body contains your actual instructions in Markdown.

**Be specific and actionable:**
Good: "Run python scripts/validate.py --input {filename} to check data format. If validation fails, common issues include: missing required fields, invalid date formats (use YYYY-MM-DD)."
Bad: "Validate the data before proceeding."

**Include error handling:**
Always add a Common Issues section. When something goes wrong, Claude needs to know what to try.

**Reference bundled resources clearly:**
"Before writing queries, consult references/api-patterns.md for rate limiting guidance, pagination patterns, and error codes."

**Use progressive disclosure in your instructions too:**
Keep SKILL.md focused on core instructions. Move detailed documentation to references/ and link to it. Keep SKILL.md under 5000 words.

**Put critical instructions at the top.** Use ## Important or ## Critical headers. Repeat key points if needed — Claude pays more attention to content early in the file.
screens.title: Writing Instructions That Work

screens.body:
Effective skill testing covers three areas:

**1. Triggering tests** — Does your skill load at the right times?
• Should trigger on obvious tasks ("Help me set up a new project")
• Should trigger on paraphrased requests ("I need to create a project")
• Should NOT trigger on unrelated topics ("What's the weather?")

**2. Functional tests** — Does it produce correct outputs?
• Valid outputs generated
• API calls succeed
• Error handling works
• Edge cases covered

**3. Performance comparison** — Is it actually better than no skill?
Compare the same task with and without the skill enabled. Count: messages needed, tool calls, failed attempts, tokens consumed.

**Pro tip from Anthropic:** Iterate on a single task before expanding. Get one challenging workflow working, extract the winning approach into a skill, then broaden to multiple use cases. This gives you faster signal than broad testing.
screens.title: Testing Your Skill

inputs.label: What should you test FIRST when building a new skill?
options: Test it on 50 different use cases at once
Iterate on a single challenging task until it succeeds
Deploy it to your whole team and collect feedback

screens.body:
Skills are living documents. After initial deployment, watch for these signals:

**Undertriggering** — your skill doesn't load when it should:
• Users manually enabling the skill
• Support questions about when to use it
• Skill doesn't activate on relevant queries
Fix: Add more detail and keyword triggers to the description.

**Overtriggering** — your skill loads when it shouldn't:
• Skill activates for irrelevant queries
• Users disabling the skill
• Confusion about the skill's purpose
Fix: Add negative triggers ("Do NOT use for..."), be more specific about scope.

**Instructions not followed** — skill loads but Claude ignores parts:
• Instructions too verbose — use bullet points, not prose
• Critical instructions buried — put them at the top
• Ambiguous language — "Make sure to validate" vs "CRITICAL: Before calling create_project, verify: project name is non-empty, at least one team member assigned, start date is not in the past"

**Model laziness** — Claude skips validation steps:
Add explicit encouragement: "Take your time. Quality is more important than speed. Do not skip validation steps."
screens.title: Iteration Signals

screens.body:
These patterns emerged from early adopters and Anthropic's internal teams:

**Pattern 1: Sequential Orchestration** — Multi-step processes in a specific order with dependencies between steps and validation at each stage.

**Pattern 2: Multi-MCP Coordination** — Workflows spanning multiple services (e.g., export from Figma, store in Drive, create tasks in Linear, notify in Slack). Clear phase separation and data passing between MCPs.

**Pattern 3: Iterative Refinement** — Output improves with iteration. Generate draft, run quality check, address issues, regenerate, repeat until threshold met.

**Pattern 4: Context-Aware Tool Selection** — Same outcome, different tools depending on context. Decision criteria drive which approach to use (e.g., large files to cloud storage, code to GitHub, docs to Notion).

**Pattern 5: Domain-Specific Intelligence** — Your skill adds specialised knowledge beyond tool access. Compliance rules, industry standards, best practices embedded in the workflow logic.
screens.title: Five Workflow Patterns

inputs.label: You want to build a skill that generates reports, checks quality, and refines until they pass. Which pattern?

screens.body:
When designing your skill, choose your approach:

**Problem-first:** "I need to set up a project workspace." Your skill orchestrates the right MCP calls in the right sequence. Users describe outcomes — the skill handles the tools.

**Tool-first:** "I have Notion MCP connected." Your skill teaches Claude the optimal workflows and best practices for that tool. Users already have access — the skill provides expertise.

Most skills lean one direction. The distinction matters because it determines how you write your instructions:

• Problem-first skills need clear step ordering, dependencies, and rollback instructions
• Tool-first skills need embedded best practices, common patterns, and domain knowledge

Knowing which framing fits your use case helps you choose the right workflow pattern from the previous screen.
screens.title: Problem-First vs Tool-First

screens.body:
**How to distribute skills:**
1. Download the skill folder
2. Zip it (if needed)
3. Upload via Claude.ai → Settings → Capabilities → Skills
4. Or place in your Claude Code skills directory

Organisations can deploy skills workspace-wide with automatic updates and centralised management.

**The skill-creator tool** is built into Claude.ai and Claude Code. It can:
• Generate skills from natural language descriptions
• Produce properly formatted SKILL.md with frontmatter
• Suggest trigger phrases and structure
• Review and flag common issues (vague descriptions, structural problems)

To use it: "Use the skill-creator skill to help me build a skill for [your use case]."

Skills are an **open standard** (like MCP) — designed to be portable across platforms, not locked to Claude.

**Position your skill around outcomes, not features:**
Good: "Enables teams to set up project workspaces in seconds — including pages, databases, and templates."
Bad: "A folder containing YAML frontmatter and Markdown instructions that calls our MCP server tools."
screens.title: Distribution and the skill-creator

screens.body:
You have the knowledge. Time to plan a real skill.

Think about a workflow you repeat often — something where you give Claude the same context and instructions regularly. That's your skill candidate.
screens.title: Now Plan Your First Skill

inputs.label: What workflow will your skill automate? (Use case, trigger, steps, result)
inputs.placeholder:
Use Case: ...
Trigger: User says ...
Steps: 1. ... 2. ... 3. ...
Result: ...

inputs.label: Which category does your skill fit?
Write the description field for your skill (what it does + when to use it + key capabilities).
